ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for a mixed roti basket; closest parent 'flatbread food product' (FOODON_03315271) used as the ontology anchor.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A00LC for unleavened bread is the closest defensible parent; no specific code exists for a mixed roti basket assortment.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: SNOMED CT 226559009 'Roti' is used as the closest parent; no specific code for a mixed roti basket assortment exists.

serving.natural_language_portion: One roti basket typically contains four pieces of assorted roti (plain, butter, pudina, and lacha), each weighing approximately 60 g, giving a total serving weight of about 240 g.

nutrient_source.title: Chapati / Roti (whole wheat, cooked on tawa) and Paratha (layered, with fat) — ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods
nutrient_source.organization: Indian Council of Medical Research – National Institute of Nutrition (ICMR-NIN)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: The roti basket contains four varieties: plain roti (chapati), butter roti (chapati with butter baste), pudina roti (chapati with mint), and lacha roti (layered paratha with fat). Nutrient values are derived as a weighted average across the four types using ICMR-NIN data: plain chapati (~300 kcal/100g, 9.5g protein, 3.7g fat, 57g carb), butter chapati (estimated +15 kcal from butter baste, ~315 kcal/100g), pudina roti (similar to plain chapati with negligible mint contribution), and lacha paratha (~330 kcal/100g, 8.5g protein, 8.5g fat, 52g carb). Weighted average across four equal portions yields the reported per-100g values. Sodium estimated from typical preparation salt levels per ICMR-NIN reference data.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of chapati and paratha — University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. (2008) International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI value exists for a mixed roti basket. GI is estimated as a weighted average of the four component types: plain chapati (GI ~62, University of Sydney GI Database), butter chapati (GI ~60, analogous to plain chapati with minor fat addition lowering GI slightly), pudina roti (GI ~62, analogous to plain chapati), and lacha paratha (GI ~66, higher fat layered paratha per Atkinson et al. 2008). Weighted average of four equal portions: (62+60+62+66)/4 = 62.5, rounded to 62. gi_category = medium (56–69 range). Glycemic load per serving = (62 × 55.5 × 240/100) / 100 = 82.7 g GL units.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content from salt used in preparation and butter baste
hypertension.guidance: A full roti basket (240 g) provides approximately 672 mg sodium; individuals with hypertension should limit portion size to one or two pieces and request reduced-salt preparation where possible.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Medium glycemic index (GI 62) and high glycemic load per full basket serving
type2_diabetes.guidance: The roti basket has a medium GI of 62 and a high glycemic load per full serving; individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should limit intake to one or two pieces per meal, pair with high-fibre vegetables or legumes to blunt postprandial glucose response, and prefer the plain or pudina varieties over the butter and lacha types.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat from butter baste and layered lacha paratha preparation
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The butter and lacha roti varieties contribute saturated fat (estimated 2.1 g per 100 g average); individuals with cardiovascular disease should favour the plain or pudina roti pieces and limit total basket consumption to reduce saturated fat and sodium intake.

llm_training.natural_language_description: A roti basket is an Indian flatbread assortment served as a shared starter or accompaniment in Indian restaurants in Singapore and across the Indian subcontinent. This basket typically contains four varieties of roti: plain roti (a thin whole-wheat chapati cooked dry on a tawa griddle), butter roti (the same chapati basted with butter after cooking for a rich, glossy finish), pudina roti (a chapati infused with fresh mint leaves, giving a fragrant, slightly herbal flavour), and lacha roti (a layered, flaky paratha made by folding fat into the dough repeatedly before cooking, producing a crisp, multi-layered texture). The breads are made primarily from whole wheat flour (atta) or a blend of atta and refined flour (maida), water, salt, and oil or butter. Nutritionally, the basket provides a moderate energy density of approximately 310 kcal per 100 g, with 9.2 g protein, 5.8 g fat, and 55.5 g carbohydrate. Dietary fibre is approximately 3.8 g per 100 g, contributed mainly by the whole wheat component. The glycemic index is estimated at 62 (medium), making portion control important for individuals managing blood glucose. Sodium content is moderate at approximately 280 mg per 100 g. The basket is vegetarian and halal-suitable but contains gluten and dairy. It is commonly served alongside curries, dal, or raita in Indian restaurants and is a culturally significant bread assortment representing the diversity of Indian flatbread traditions.
common_user_queries: How many calories in a roti basket?
What types of roti are in a roti basket?
Is roti basket suitable for diabetics?
What is the difference between plain roti and butter roti?

confidence_reason: No exact match for a mixed roti basket assortment exists in ICMR-NIN or any other approved primary source. Nutrient values are derived as a weighted average of four component roti types (plain chapati, butter chapati, pudina roti, lacha paratha) using ICMR-NIN reference data for each type. GI is estimated as a weighted average from University of Sydney GI Database values for analogous individual roti types. Confidence is therefore medium rather than high.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are a weighted average across four equal-portion roti types (plain, butter, pudina, lacha) derived from ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods reference data. No single approved-source entry exists for a mixed roti basket. 2. vitamin_b12_mcg is 0.0 because roti is a plant-based bread product containing no animal-derived vitamin B12; this is naturally zero for all wheat flatbread products. 3. vitamin_d_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the ICMR-NIN source for chapati or paratha products. 4. selenium_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the ICMR-NIN source for chapati or paratha products. 5. GI of 62 is an analogous estimate weighted across four roti types using University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. (2008) International Tables of GI and GL. 6. Glycemic load per serving calculated as (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (62 × 55.5 × 2.4) / 100 = 82.7. 7. Serving size of 240 g represents one full basket of four rotis at approximately 60 g each, consistent with typical restaurant portions. 8. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, calcium_mg, iron_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, and water_g are estimated from ICMR-NIN component data and should be treated as indicative rather than analytically confirmed values.

improvement_actions_taken: Derived weighted-average nutrient values from ICMR-NIN component data for four roti types.
Documented vitamin_b12_mcg = 0.0 as naturally zero for plant-based wheat flatbread.
Documented vitamin_d_mcg = -1 and selenium_mcg = -1 as not reported in ICMR-NIN source.
Estimated GI as weighted average of four component roti types from University of Sydney GI Database.
Added full multilingual local_names and aliases for Chinese (simplified and traditional), Malay, Tamil, and Hindi.
Verified aliases_by_language and flat aliases list for exact string consistency.
Set all ontology match_type values to allowed values only (closest_parent).
Ensured gi_category = 'medium' consistent with GI = 62 (56–69 range per validator thresholds).
